excerpt: in any prayer in which the recitation is uttered aloud, Bismillāh al-Raḥmān al-Raḥīm too must be pronounced aloud, and that here taqiyya is not permitted under any circumstance.
source_authority: Chief Fatimid qāḍī under al-Muʿizz; foundational Ismāʿīlī jurist
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣalāt, chapters on the recitation of al-Fātiḥa and the audibility of Bismillāh

excerpt: He has originated the spiritual beings ex nihilo — without any pre-existing matter (lā min shayʾ) — which is precisely what ulūhiyya entails.
source_location_hint: Yanābīʿ on the uniqueness of the divine Name and the doctrine of ibdāʿ (origination without antecedent matter)

excerpt: the sons of the Messenger — the Imams who have succeeded him in his command — are themselves the Names of God: what they declare lawful is lawful, and what they declare unlawful is unlawful.
source_authority: Yemeni Ismāʿīlī dāʿī of the early Fatimid period
source_location_hint: Sections on the taʾwīl of the divine Names and on the imamate as the locus of the Greatest Name

excerpt: every lower limit, by comparison with the limit above it — the ḥadd ʿālī — is a muḥmil and so without meaning in itself; yet by comparison with what stands below it — the ḥadd dānī — that same limit is itself a bearer of meaning.
source_authority: Chief Fatimid dāʿī under al-Ḥākim bi-Amr Allāh
source_location_hint: Mashāriʿ on the ḥudūd and the cascading doctrine of taʾyīd from upper to lower limits

excerpt: In every affair of this universe, religious and worldly alike, God has appointed instrumental causes (Dār al-Asbāb).
source_location_hint: Sections treating the corporeal world as the Abode of Causes and the necessity of mediating means

excerpt: The doctrine of the ten ḥudūd al-dīn — five spiritual (al-ʿaql, al-nafs, al-jadd, al-fatḥ, al-khayāl) and five corporeal (al-nāṭiq, al-asās, al-imām, al-ḥujja, al-dāʿī) — through which the believer recognizes the Originator (Mubdiʿ Taʿālā) and discerns His incomparability with any one of them.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the spiritual and corporeal ḥudūd and their correspondences (al-mashraʿ al-sābiʿ).

excerpt: The taʾwīl method by which apparent traditions concerning fire and hell are read as referring to the opponents of the Ahl al-Bayt and the burning of doubt and confusion they sow in their followers — quelled before the simple, evidence-bearing question of the Imam's mustajīb.
source_location_hint: Majālis treating the taʾwīl of nār, jaḥīm, and the contest between bāṭinī and ẓāhirī knowledge.

excerpt: The Prophet — and, in his absence, the Imam — is the Name of God (ism Allāh) in his age, through whom alone God is recognized, just as a man is known by the name he bears.
source_location_hint: Discussions of the Nāṭiq and Imam as the manifest Name of God and locus of recognition.

excerpt: The doctrine that the whole of revelation is progressively gathered — from the books of the prophets into the Qurʾan, into Sūrat al-Fātiḥa, into the Bismillāh, into the Bāʾ, and finally into the single point beneath it, identified with the walāya of ʿAlī.
source_location_hint: Taʾwīl of al-Fātiḥa and of the Bismillāh, with the saying anā nuqṭat taḥt al-bāʾ attributed to Amīr al-Muʾminīn.

excerpt: The classification of the Arabic alphabet into nūrānī (luminous) and ẓulmānī (dark) letters, with the bāʾ as the chief of the dark letters and the closest to the luminous — and the doctrine of the lāsiq, the binder linking ʿālam al-ḥayāt to the encompassing sphere.
source_location_hint: Cosmological exposition of the luminous and dark letters and the role of the bāʾ in the descent and ascent of the ḥurūf.

excerpt: The hierarchy of ḥudūd in which the visible Imam serves as ḥadd dānī, the lower limit through which the seeker ascends to the ḥadd ʿālī, the upper limit.
source_authority: ḥujja of Iraq under the Fatimid caliph al-Ḥākim
source_location_hint: discussion of the hierarchy of ḥudūd and the relation of upper and lower limits

excerpt: The inner reality of the Imam exceeds the unaided capacity of human perception; the seeker approaches him through his manifest aspect, not his concealed one.
source_authority: dāʿī al-duʿāt under the Fatimid caliph al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: discussion of the Imam's manifest (ẓāhir) and concealed (bāṭin) aspects

excerpt: The Prophet's saying 'anā wa ʿAlī min nūr wāḥid'—'ʿAlī and I are of one light'—is cited as proof of the shared luminous origin of nāṭiq and asās.
source_authority: dāʿī al-duʿāt under the Fatimid caliph al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: majālis citing the 'one light' tradition concerning the Prophet and ʿAlī

excerpt: The taʾwīl of letters in which bāʾ and the dot beneath it figure the ranks of nāṭiq and asās, and through which the names of God are unveiled.
source_authority: chief qāḍī under the Fatimid caliph al-Muʿizz
source_location_hint: discussion of letter symbolism and the taʾwīl of the basmala
